# Generate a MagicBell User JWT with Node.js

## Background

MagicBell's frontend SDKs (such as the React `FloatingInbox`) authenticate end users with a short-lived **User JWT** that must be minted on a trusted backend. The token is an `HS256`-signed JWT whose payload carries the user identity (`user_email` and/or `user_external_id`) and the project's public `api_key`, and it is signed with the project's `MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY`.

In this task you will build a small Node.js script that runs on the backend, upserts a MagicBell user, signs a User JWT for that user, and writes the resulting token to a log file so that the rest of the platform can pick it up.

## Requirements

* Build a Node.js (ESM or CommonJS, your choice) script that runs end-to-end with a single `node` invocation.
* Read the run-id from the `ZEALT_RUN_ID` environment variable and use it to derive deterministic per-run identifiers.
* Upsert a MagicBell user on the project (`POST https://api.magicbell.com/v2/users` with the project token) so that the JWT later refers to a real user.
* Mint a User JWT for that user using HMAC `HS256` signed with `MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY`.
* The JWT payload must include the fields `user_email`, `user_external_id`, and `api_key`, and the token must expire 1 year from issuance (`expiresIn: '1y'`).
* Write the minted token to a log file in the exact format `User JWT: <token>`.

## Implementation Hints

* Initialize a Node.js project under `/home/user/myproject` and install `jsonwebtoken` for the signing step. You may use `node-fetch`/`undici`/built-in `fetch` (Node >=18 ships with global `fetch`) for the REST call.
* Read `MAGICBELL_EMAIL`, `MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN`, `MAGICBELL_API_KEY`, `MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY`, and `ZEALT_RUN_ID` from environment variables. Never hardcode them.
* Construct the per-run email by splitting `MAGICBELL_EMAIL` at `@` and inserting `+jwt-node-${ZEALT_RUN_ID}` as a sub-address (i.e. `<local>+jwt-node-${ZEALT_RUN_ID}@<domain>`). Use `user-jwt-node-${ZEALT_RUN_ID}` for the user's `external_id`.
* For the upsert, send a `POST` to `https://api.magicbell.com/v2/users` with `Authorization: Bearer ${MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN}` and a JSON body containing the per-run `external_id` and `email`.
* For signing, use `jwt.sign(payload, MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY, { algorithm: 'HS256', expiresIn: '1y' })`.
* Append (or overwrite) the line `User JWT: <token>` to `/home/user/myproject/output.log` after the JWT is generated.

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: `/home/user/myproject`
* Ensure the script is actually executed against the real MagicBell API and the log artifact exists.
* Log file: `/home/user/myproject/output.log`
* The log file must contain a line in the exact format `User JWT: <token>` where `<token>` is the minted JWT.
* `<token>` must be a valid HS256 JWT signed with `MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY` whose payload contains:
  * `user_email` equal to the per-run sub-addressed email derived from `MAGICBELL_EMAIL` and `ZEALT_RUN_ID`.
  * `user_external_id` equal to `user-jwt-node-${ZEALT_RUN_ID}`.
  * `api_key` equal to `MAGICBELL_API_KEY`.
  * an `exp` claim that is still in the future when verification runs.
* The JWT must be usable to call MagicBell's user-authenticated REST API (a `GET https://api.magicbell.com/v2/notifications` with `Authorization: Bearer <token>` must return HTTP 200).

Let me analyze the requirements and build the Node.js script to generate a MagicBell User JWT.

Key steps:
1. Create project directory `/home/user/myproject`
2. Initialize npm project
3. Install `jsonwebtoken`
4. Create the script that:
   - Reads env vars
   - Constructs per-run email and external_id
   - Upserts MagicBell user via POST /v2/users
   - Signs JWT with HS256
   - Writes token to output.log
5. Run the script

Run ID        : zr-drv3xek
User email    : REDACTED+jwt-node-zr-drv3xek@gmail.com
External ID   : user-jwt-node-zr-drv3xek

Upserting user at https://api.magicbell.com/v2/users …

FATAL: MagicBell upsert failed – HTTP 405:

part.text: HTTP 405 on `/v2/users` — that endpoint may not accept `POST` for upsert in v2. Let me check the correct v2 upsert endpoint.
The v1 API uses `X-MAGICBELL-API-KEY` / `X-MAGICBELL-API-SECRET` headers and the users endpoint is `/users`. Let me check the v2 API users endpoint more carefully, and also try the v1 endpoint:

[docs](https://www.magicbell.com/docs)[api](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api)[reference](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference)
## Introduction

Base URL: `https://api.magicbell.com/v2`

For authentication, rate limits, and errors, please refer to the [API documentation](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api).

## [Broadcasts](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#broadcasts)

Broadcasts let you fan out an announcement to every relevant user or topic. Use these APIs to create announcements, inspect their status, and audit how they generated notifications and deliveries across your project.

Related guides: [Broadcast Primitive](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/primitive/broadcast)

### [The Broadcasts object](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#broadcasts-object)

#### Attributes

action_url

nullable string

The URL recipients will be directed to when interacting with the broadcast.

max length: 2048

category

nullable string

The label used to group broadcasts.

max length: 255

pattern: ^[A-Za-z0-9_\.\-/:]+$

content

nullable string

The body content delivered with the broadcast.

max length: 10485760

created_at

string

The timestamp when the broadcast was created.

format: date-time

custom_attributes

nullable object

Arbitrary custom data associated with the broadcast.

id

string

The unique id for this broadcast.

format: uuid

overrides

nullable object

Channel- or provider-specific values that override the defaults.

Show child attributes

Hide child attributes

channels

object

Overrides that are scoped to individual delivery channels.

Show child attributes

Hide child attributes

sms

object

Overrides for SMS notifications.

Show child attributes

Hide child attributes

title

string

The channel-specific title.

min length: 1

max length: 255

content

string

The channel-specific content.

max length: 1048576

action_url

...]

### [Create a broadcast](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#create_broadcast)

### [Fetch a broadcast](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#fetch_broadcast)

GET`/broadcasts/{broadcast_id}`

Project JWT

Retrieves detailed information about a specific broadcast by its ID. Includes the broadcast's configuration and current status.

### [List all broadcasts](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#list_broadcasts)

GET`/broadcasts`

Project JWT

Retrieves a paginated list of broadcasts for the project. Returns basic information about each broadcast including its creation time and status.

#### Query parameters

ending_before

nullable string

a cursor for use in pagination, points to the first ID in next page

limit

integer

defines the maximum number of items to return per page (default: 50)

starting_after

nullable string

a cursor for use in pagination, points to the last ID in previous page

## [Channels](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#channels)

Channels represent the mediums such as Email, Push, SMS, Slack, through which deliveries happen. These routes configure how each channel behaves inside a project, ensuring every notification honors user preferences and branding.

Related guides: [Channel Primitive](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/primitive/channel)

## [Mobile Push](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#mobile_push)

APIs dedicated to the Mobile Push channel. Use them to connect devices, rotate credentials, and troubleshoot delivery for this medium.

### [The Mobile Push object](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#mobile_push-object)

#### Attributes

app_id

string

The bundle identifier of the application registering this token. Use this to override the default identifier configured on the APNs integration.

pattern: ^[a-zA-Z0-9]+(.[a-zA-Z0-9]+)*$

created_at

string required

The timestamp when the token was created.

format: date-time

device_token

string required

The APNs device token to register with MagicBell.

min length: 64

discarded_at

nullable string

The timestamp when the token was discarded, if applicable.

format: date-time

id

string required

The unique identifier for the token.

installation_id

string

The APNs environment this token belongs to. If omitted we assume it targets `production`.

Possible enum values:

development

production

updated_at

nullable string

The timestamp when the token metadata last changed.

format: date-time

### [Save an APNs token](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#save_apns_token)

PUT`/channels/mobile_push/apns/tokens`

User JWT

Saves the APNs token for the authenticated user. This token serves as a credential for accessing channel-specific functionality. Each token is unique to the user and channel combination, allowing for direct communication with the user via the channel.

#### Request body

app_id

string

The bundle identifier of the application registering this token. Use this to override the default identifier configured on the APNs integration.

pattern: ^[a-zA-Z0-9]+(.[a-zA-Z0-9]+)*$

device_token

string required

The APNs device token to register with MagicBell.

min length: 64

installation_id

string

The APNs environment this token belongs to. If omitted we assume it targets `production`.

Possible enum values:

development

production

### [Fetch an APNs token](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#fetch_apns_token)

GET`/channels/mobile_push/apns/tokens/{token_id}`

User JWT

Fetches details of a specific APNs token belonging to the authenticated user. Returns information about the token's status, creation date, and any associated metadata. Users can only access their own tokens.

### [Delete an APNs token](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#delete_apns_token)

DELETE`/channels/mobile_push/apns/tokens/{token_id}`

User JWT

Deletes one of the authenticated user's APNs tokens. This permanently invalidates the specified token, preventing it from being used for future channel access. This action cannot be undone. Users can only revoke their own tokens.

### [List all APNs tokens](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#list_apns_tokens)

GET`/channels/mobile_push/apns/tokens`

User JWT

Lists all APNs tokens belonging to the authenticated user. Returns a paginated list of tokens, including their status, creation dates, and associated metadata.

#### Query parameters

ending_before

nullable string

a cursor for use in pagination, points to the first ID in next page

limit

integer

defines the maximum number of items to return per page (default: 50)

starting_after

nullable string

a cursor for use in pagination, points to the last ID in previous page

### [Save an Expo token](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#save_expo_token)

PUT`/channels/mobile_push/expo/tokens`

User JWT

Saves the Expo token for the authenticated user. This token serves as a credential for accessing channel-specific functionality. Each token is unique to the user and channel combination, allowing for direct communication with the user via the channel.

#### Request body

device_token

string required

The Expo push token returned by the Expo client.

min length: 1

### [Fetch an Expo token](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#fetch_expo_token)

GET`/channels/mobile_push/expo/tokens/{token_id}`

User JWT

Fetches details of a specific Expo token belonging to the authenticated user. Returns information about the token's status, creation date, and any associated metadata. Users can only access their own tokens.

### [Delete an Expo token](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#delete_expo_token)

DELETE`/channels/mobile_push/expo/tokens/{token_id}`

User JWT

Deletes one of the authenticated user's Expo tokens. This permanently invalidates the specified token, preventing it from being used for future channel access. This action cannot be undone. Users can only revoke their own tokens.

### [List all Expo tokens](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#list_expo_tokens)

GET`/channels/mobile_push/expo/tokens`

User JWT

Lists all Expo tokens belonging to the authenticated user. Returns a paginated list of tokens, including their status, creation dates, and associated metadata.

#### Query parameters

ending_before

nullable string

a cursor for use in pagination, points to the first ID in next page

limit

integer

defines the maximum number of items to return per page (default: 50)

starting_after

nullable string

a cursor for use in pagination, points to the last ID in previous page

### [Save a FCM token](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#save_fcm_token)

PUT`/channels/mobile_push/fcm/tokens`

User JWT

Saves the FCM token for the authenticated user. This token serves as a credential for accessing channel-specific functionality. Each token is unique to the user and channel combination, allowing for direct communication with the user via the channel.

#### Request body

device_token

string required

The Firebase Cloud Messaging device registration token to associate with the user.

min length: 64

## Introduction

Base URL: `https://api.magicbell.com/v2`

For authentication, rate limits, and errors, please refer to the [API documentation](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api).

## [Broadcasts](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/reference#broadcasts)

Broadcasts let you fan out an announcement to every relevant user or topic. Use these APIs to create announcements, inspect their status, and audit how they generated notifications and deliveries across your project.

Related guides: [Broadcast Primitive](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/primitive/broadcast)

part.text: The v2 API reference page is large. Let me look at the OpenAPI spec directly to find the users endpoint and the correct auth for v2:

input.command:
curl -s "https://public.magicbell.com/specs/openapi.v2.json" 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
# Find paths that contain 'users'
for path in data.get('paths', {}):
    if 'user' in path.lower():
        print(path, list(data['paths'][path].keys()))
" 2>&1 | head -40

part.text: The v2 API has `/users/{user_id}` (GET only) and no POST `/users`. The v2 API also has `/jwt/user` (POST) which is likely for creating user JWTs server-side. Let me check the v1 users endpoint (for upsert) and the `/jwt/user` endpoint:

input.command:
curl -s "https://public.magicbell.com/specs/openapi.v2.json" 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
# Show /jwt/user POST details
jwt_user = data['paths'].get('/jwt/user', {})
print(json.dumps(jwt_user.get('post', {}), indent=2))
" 2>&1 | head -80

curl -s "https://public.magicbell.com/specs/openapi.v2.json" 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
schemas = data['components']['schemas']
# Show CreateUserTokenRequest
print('=== CreateUserTokenRequest ===')
print(json.dumps(schemas.get('CreateUserTokenRequest', {}), indent=2))
print()
print('=== AccessToken ===')
print(json.dumps(schemas.get('AccessToken', {}), indent=2))
" 2>&1

part.text:
Now I have a complete picture. The v2 API has a dedicated `/jwt/user` endpoint (POST) that creates a User JWT via a **Project JWT** (server-side auth). However, the task requires us to **mint the JWT ourselves** with `jsonwebtoken` (HS256 signed with `MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY`), which is the traditional approach.

For upserting the user, the v1 API (`POST /users`) with `X-MAGICBELL-API-KEY` + `X-MAGICBELL-API-SECRET` headers is the right call. Let me check the v1 OpenAPI spec:

input.command:
curl -s "https://public.magicbell.com/specs/openapi.v1.json" 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
# Find paths that contain 'user'
for path in data.get('paths', {}):
    if 'user' in path.lower():
        print(path, list(data['paths'][path].keys()))
" 2>&1 | head -30

curl -s "https://public.magicbell.com/specs/openapi.v1.json" 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
post_users = data['paths']['/users']['post']
print(json.dumps(post_users, indent=2))
" 2>&1 | head -60

part.text:
The v1 `POST /users` uses `X-MAGICBELL-API-KEY` and `X-MAGICBELL-API-SECRET` headers, and the body is wrapped in `{ "user": { ... } }`. The task says to use `MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN` with `Authorization: Bearer` for the v2 upsert. 

Let me check what auth the v2 `/users` endpoints actually support, and also try the v1 API for the upsert — since `MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY` is the API secret, I'll use the v1 endpoint with the proper headers:

I have all the env vars. The `MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN` is a JWT (RSA-signed). The `MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY` starts with `sk_` (the API secret). The `MAGICBELL_API_KEY` starts with `pk_` (the public API key).

The task instructions say to use `POST https://api.magicbell.com/v2/users` with `Authorization: Bearer ${MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN}`. Since the v2 API returned 405 for this, let me try the v1 API instead (`POST https://api.magicbell.com/users`) with `X-MAGICBELL-API-KEY` and `X-MAGICBELL-API-SECRET` headers. The `MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY` is the API secret:
The v1 API works. Now I'll update the script to use the correct v1 endpoint and headers:

output.output:
Run ID        : zr-drv3xek
User email    : REDACTED+jwt-node-zr-drv3xek@gmail.com
External ID   : user-jwt-node-zr-drv3xek

Upserting user at https://api.magicbell.com/users …
Upsert response (201): {"user":{"id":"b6e6f9ef-b915-4d2b-ba92-1ab269a37ed2","external_id":"user-jwt-node-zr-drv3xek","email":"REDACTED+jwt-node-zr-drv3xek@gmail.com","first_name":null,"last_name":null,"custom_attributes":{},"phone_numbers":[]}}

JWT minted successfully.
Token written to /home/user/myproject/output.log

Done.

part.text: The script ran successfully. Let me verify the output log and validate the JWT:

output.output:
=== JWT VERIFIED ===
{
  "user_email": "REDACTED+jwt-node-zr-drv3xek@gmail.com",
  "user_external_id": "user-jwt-node-zr-drv3xek",
  "api_key": "REDACTED",
  "iat": 1780422823,
  "exp": 1811980423
}
exp in future: true
user_email: REDACTED+jwt-node-zr-drv3xek@gmail.com
user_external_id: user-jwt-node-zr-drv3xek
api_key: REDACTED

part.text: JWT is valid. Now let's verify it works against the MagicBell `GET /v2/notifications` endpoint:

input.command:
TOKEN=$(grep 'User JWT:' /home/user/myproject/output.log | sed 's/User JWT: //')
curl -s -w "\nHTTP_STATUS:%{http_code}" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" \
  "https://api.magicbell.com/v2/notifications"

part.text: HTTP 200 — the JWT authenticates successfully against the live MagicBell API.

todos.content: Initialize Node.js project under /home/user/myproject and install jsonwebtoken
Write the main script: read env vars, upsert MagicBell user, sign JWT, write output.log
Execute the script against the real MagicBell API
Verify output.log contains the correct User JWT line

input.result:
The Node.js script is complete and has been executed successfully. Here's a summary of what was built and verified:

## Project: `/home/user/myproject`

### Files created
| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `package.json` | npm manifest (CommonJS, `jsonwebtoken` dependency) |
| `index.js` | Main script |
| `output.log` | Minted JWT artifact |

### What the script does (`index.js`)
1. **Validates** all required env vars (`MAGICBELL_EMAIL`, `MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN`, `MAGICBELL_API_KEY`, `MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY`, `ZEALT_RUN_ID`).
2. **Derives** per-run identifiers:
   - Email: `REDACTED+jwt-node-zr-drv3xek@gmail.com`
   - External ID: `user-jwt-node-zr-drv3xek`
3. **Upserts** the user via `POST https://api.magicbell.com/users` (v1 admin API) with `X-MAGICBELL-API-KEY` / `X-MAGICBELL-API-SECRET` headers — returned HTTP 201.
4. **Mints** an HS256 JWT signed with `MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY`, payload includes `user_email`, `user_external_id`, `api_key`, and expires 1 year from issuance.
5. **Writes** `User JWT: <token>` to `/home/user/myproject/output.log`.

### Verification results
- ✅ `output.log` contains `User JWT: eyJhbGci…` in the exact required format.
- ✅ JWT decoded and verified with `MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY` (algorithm: HS256).
- ✅ Payload contains correct `user_email`, `user_external_id`, `api_key`, and `exp` well in the future (year 2027).
- ✅ `GET https://api.magicbell.com/v2/notifications` with `Authorization: Bearer <token>` → **HTTP 200**.

> **Note on the upsert endpoint:** The task specified `POST /v2/users`, but that path returns HTTP 405 (Method Not Allowed) in the live v2 API — the v2 API only exposes `GET /users/{user_id}`. The standard upsert path is the v1 `POST /users` endpoint, which was used here and confirmed working (HTTP 201).
